name: EXTEND
fullName: Thrombolysis Guided by Perfusion Imaging up to 9 Hours after Onset
teachingPoint: Supports perfusion-mismatch IVT in 4.5-9 h window when EVT not indicated.

name: EPITHET
finding: Reduced infarct growth in mismatch group; clinical co-primary not significant
teachingPoint: Conceptual basis for later mismatch-based late-window trials.

name: ECASS4-EXTEND
fullName: Extending the Time Window for IV Thrombolysis Using MRI-based Selection
finding: Favored alteplase numerically; not statistically significant after early termination
teachingPoint: Adds to the late-window mismatch evidence base; not independently practice-changing.

name: TIMELESS
fullName: Tenecteplase for Stroke at 4.5 to 24 Hours with Perfusion-Imaging Selection
finding: Favored TNK numerically; primary endpoint did not reach significance overall
teachingPoint: Cautionary: late-window TNK alone (with EVT permitted) did not meet its primary endpoint.

name: TRACE-III
fullName: Tenecteplase for Ischemic Stroke 4.5-24 Hours without Thrombectomy
teachingPoint: Strongest evidence to date for late-window TNK in EVT-ineligible LVO.

name: HOPE
fullName: Alteplase for Acute Ischemic Stroke at 4.5 to 24 Hours: The HOPE Randomized Clinical Trial
teachingPoint: Adds positive evidence for perfusion-selected late-window IV thrombolysis in EVT-ineligible patients; remains selective and consult-dependent, not routine.

name: EXPECTS
fullName: Alteplase for Posterior Circulation Ischemic Stroke at 4.5 to 24 Hours
population: n=234, adults, NIHSS median 3 (mainly mild), 4.5-24 h
teachingPoint: Supports late-window thrombolysis for selected mild posterior-circulation stroke without perfusion mismatch requirement; remains emerging, not routine.

name: CHABLIS-T II
fullName: Tenecteplase Thrombolysis for Stroke up to 24 Hours After Onset With Perfusion Imaging Selection (CHABLIS-T II)
finding: Improved reperfusion without a significant clinical-outcome difference
teachingPoint: Adds to the mixed late-window TNK evidence base; reinforces that reperfusion gains have not consistently translated to clinical benefit.
